recipes.title: Tanzanian Pilau
recipes.description: Aromatic Swahili coast rice with beef, whole spices, and caramelised onions, central to Tanzanian celebrations.
steps: Rinse the rice, soak for 20 minutes, and drain.
Heat the oil in a heavy pot and fry the onions over medium heat until very deeply caramelised, about 18 minutes.
Add the cumin seeds, cardamom, cinnamon, cloves, and peppercorns and toast for 1 minute.
Stir in the garlic and ginger, then add the beef and brown all over.
Add the potatoes and salt, stir, and pour in the beef stock.
Cover and simmer until the meat is tender, about 45 minutes.
Stir in the rice, ensuring the liquid sits 2 cm above the grains.
Cover tightly, lower the heat to minimum, and steam undisturbed for 20 minutes.
Rest off heat for 10 minutes, then fluff with a fork and serve with kachumbari.

recipes.title: Mishkaki
recipes.description: Tanzanian charcoal-grilled meat skewers marinated in lemon, garlic, and warm spices, sold at every coastal night market.
steps: Whisk garlic, ginger, lemon juice, oil, cumin, paprika, coriander, chilli powder, and salt into a marinade.
Toss the beef cubes in the marinade and refrigerate for at least 4 hours, ideally overnight.
Soak wooden skewers in water for 30 minutes if using.
Thread 4-5 cubes of beef onto each skewer, packed snugly.
Prepare a charcoal grill with hot embers.
Grill the skewers over direct heat for 3 minutes per side, turning twice, until charred outside and just pink inside.
Rest the skewers for 3 minutes off the heat.
Serve hot with kachumbari, chapati, or roasted cassava.

recipes.title: Mtori
recipes.description: Kilimanjaro region soup of green bananas simmered with beef into a creamy, comforting broth.

ingredients.name: green cooking bananas, peeled and chopped

steps: Place the beef in a heavy pot with the water and bring to a gentle boil.
Skim the surface and simmer covered for 45 minutes until the beef is tender.
In a separate pan, heat the oil and sauté the onion until soft.
Add the garlic and cumin and cook briefly, then stir in the tomato and cook into a paste.
Tip the onion mixture into the beef pot.
Add the green bananas and salt, top up with water if needed to cover, and simmer for 25 minutes until the bananas are very soft.
Use a wooden masher or stick blender briefly to break down half of the bananas, leaving some chunks for texture.
Stir in the coriander and adjust salt before serving in deep bowls.

recipes.title: Wali wa Nazi
recipes.description: Coastal Tanzanian coconut rice cooked in fresh coconut milk for a fragrant, slightly sweet side.
steps: Rinse the rice in cold water until the water runs clear, then drain.
Combine the coconut milk and water in a pot and bring to a gentle boil.
Stir in the salt and oil.
Add the drained rice and stir once.
Lower the heat to minimum, cover tightly, and steam for 18 minutes without lifting the lid.
Turn off the heat and let stand covered for 10 minutes.
Fluff with a fork and serve with fish curry, beans, or grilled meats.

recipes.title: Ndizi Kaanga
recipes.description: Tanzanian fried plantains, golden and caramelised, served as a side or snack.
steps: Choose plantains with deep yellow skins flecked with black for natural sweetness.
Peel the plantains and slice them on a sharp diagonal into 1 cm thick ovals.
Heat the oil in a wide pan over medium heat to about 170°C.
Fry the plantain slices in a single layer for 2-3 minutes per side until deeply golden and caramelised at the edges.
Work in batches to avoid crowding the pan.
Remove with a slotted spoon and drain on paper towels.
Sprinkle very lightly with salt while still hot.
Serve warm with beans, rice, or stew.

recipes.title: Supu ya Ndizi
recipes.description: Tanzanian green banana soup with beef, similar to mtori but lighter and brothier, popular in the north.
steps: Place the beef bones in a large pot with the water and bring to a boil.
Skim the foam and simmer gently for 75 minutes to make a rich broth.
Lift out the bones, strip the meat, and return the meat to the pot.
In a small pan, heat the oil and sauté the onion, garlic, and tomato into a paste, then stir into the broth.
Add the green bananas and carrot.
Season with salt and black pepper and simmer for 25 minutes until the bananas are tender.
Stir in the lemon juice and coriander.
Serve hot in deep bowls with bread on the side.

recipes.title: Mchicha
recipes.description: Tanzanian braised amaranth greens with onion, tomato, peanut, and coconut milk.
steps: Heat the oil in a wide pan and sauté the onion until soft and translucent.
Add the garlic and cook for 1 minute.
Stir in the tomatoes and cook until they break down into a sauce, about 5 minutes.
Whisk the peanut butter into the coconut milk until smooth, then add to the pan.
Bring to a simmer and cook for 3 minutes to thicken slightly.
Add the amaranth leaves a handful at a time, tossing to wilt.
Season with salt, cover, and steam for 5 minutes.
Uncover, stir, and simmer for another 3 minutes to bind the sauce. Serve with ugali or rice.

recipes.title: Zanzibar Mix
recipes.description: Iconic Zanzibari street food: a tangy chickpea and potato soup topped with fried bhajia, crisps, and chutney.
steps: Heat the oil in a pot and sauté the onion until soft.
Add the garlic, turmeric, and cumin and stir for 1 minute.
Add the cubed potatoes and water and bring to a boil.
Simmer for 15 minutes until the potatoes are tender.
Stir in the chickpeas and tamarind paste, season with salt, and simmer for another 10 minutes until the broth thickens slightly.
Lightly mash a few potatoes against the side of the pot to thicken the soup.
Stir in the lemon juice and adjust salt.
Ladle into bowls and top each with bhajia, crushed crisps, coriander, and a spoonful of green chutney just before serving.

recipes.title: Vitumbua
recipes.description: Coconut and cardamom rice cakes from the Swahili coast, fried in special domed pans and eaten with chai.
steps: Dissolve the yeast and 1 tsp of the sugar in the warm water and rest for 10 minutes until foamy.
In a bowl, whisk the rice flour, remaining sugar, cardamom, and salt.
Pour in the coconut milk and yeast mixture and whisk to a smooth, lump-free batter.
Cover and rest in a warm place for 1 hour until bubbly on the surface.
Heat a vitumbua pan or a small ebelskiver pan over medium heat and brush each well with oil.
Pour batter into each well to about three-quarters full.
Cook for 2-3 minutes until the bottoms are golden and lacy, then flip with a skewer and cook the other side for 2 minutes.
Lift out, drain briefly, and serve warm with spiced chai.
